---
title: "Chapter 37 — 🚨 Anomaly Detection"
page-layout: full
toc: true
toc-location: left
toc-title: "In this chapter"
toc-depth: 2
toc-expand: true
---

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 36 · ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting](ch36.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [38 · 🧭 Search & Problem Solving →](ch38.html)

<details class="ig-jump">
<summary>📚 Jump to any chapter</summary>
<p class="ig-era">🌐 Foundations</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch01.html">01 &middot; 🌐 The Landscape of AI</a></li>
<li><a href="ch02.html">02 &middot; 🧮 Mathematics for Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch03.html">03 &middot; 🔄 The Machine Learning Process</a></li>
<li><a href="ch04.html">04 &middot; 🧹 Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧩 Classical Machine Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch05.html">05 &middot; 📈 Linear & Logistic Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch06.html">06 &middot; 📍 Instance-Based & Probabilistic Classifiers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch07.html">07 &middot; 🛡️ Support Vector Machines & Kernels</a></li>
<li><a href="ch08.html">08 &middot; 🌳 Decision Trees & Ensemble Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch09.html">09 &middot; 🔮 Clustering & Unsupervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch10.html">10 &middot; 🗜️ Dimensionality Reduction & Manifold Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch11.html">11 &middot; 🎯 Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🎲 Probabilistic & Statistical ML</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch12.html">12 &middot; 🎲 Bayesian Machine Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch13.html">13 &middot; 🕸️ Probabilistic Graphical Models</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🧠 Deep Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch14.html">14 &middot; 🧠 Neural Network Fundamentals</a></li>
<li><a href="ch15.html">15 &middot; ⚙️ Training Deep Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch16.html">16 &middot; 🖼️ Convolutional Neural Networks</a></li>
<li><a href="ch17.html">17 &middot; 🔁 Recurrent & Sequence Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch18.html">18 &middot; ⚡ Attention & Transformers</a></li>
<li><a href="ch19.html">19 &middot; 🪞 Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch20.html">20 &middot; 🎨 Generative Models: GANs, VAEs & Flows</a></li>
<li><a href="ch21.html">21 &middot; 🌫️ Diffusion Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch22.html">22 &middot; 🔗 Graph Neural Networks</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🕹️ Reinforcement Learning</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch23.html">23 &middot; 🕹️ Reinforcement Learning Foundations</a></li>
<li><a href="ch24.html">24 &middot; 🎰 Model-Free Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch25.html">25 &middot; 🤖 Deep Reinforcement Learning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch26.html">26 &middot; ♟️ Multi-Agent RL & Game Theory</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🗣️ Language, Vision & Modalities</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch27.html">27 &middot; 👁️ Computer Vision</a></li>
<li><a href="ch28.html">28 &middot; 💬 Natural Language Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch29.html">29 &middot; 🔊 Speech & Audio Processing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch30.html">30 &middot; 📚 Large Language Models</a></li>
<li><a href="ch31.html">31 &middot; 🎚️ Prompting, Fine-Tuning & Alignment</a></li>
<li><a href="ch32.html">32 &middot; 🔎 Retrieval-Augmented Generation & Vector Search</a></li>
<li><a href="ch33.html">33 &middot; 🦾 AI Agents & Tool Use</a></li>
<li><a href="ch34.html">34 &middot; 🌈 Multimodal AI</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🛠️ Applied ML Systems</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch35.html">35 &middot; 🛒 Recommender Systems</a></li>
<li><a href="ch36.html">36 &middot; ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting</a></li>
<li><a href="ch37.html">37 &middot; 🚨 Anomaly Detection</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">📚 Classical & Symbolic AI</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch38.html">38 &middot; 🧭 Search & Problem Solving</a></li>
<li><a href="ch39.html">39 &middot; 📖 Knowledge Representation & Reasoning</a></li>
<li><a href="ch40.html">40 &middot; 🗺️ Planning, Constraint Satisfaction & Game Playing</a></li>
<li><a href="ch41.html">41 &middot; 🧬 Evolutionary Computation & Metaheuristics</a></li>
</ul>
<p class="ig-era">🚀 Production, Responsibility & Frontier</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="ch42.html">42 &middot; 🔧 MLOps & LLMOps</a></li>
<li><a href="ch43.html">43 &middot; 🚀 AI Infrastructure & Efficient Inference</a></li>
<li><a href="ch44.html">44 &middot; 🔍 Explainable AI & Interpretability</a></li>
<li><a href="ch45.html">45 &middot; 🧷 Causal Inference & Causal ML</a></li>
<li><a href="ch46.html">46 &middot; ⚖️ AI Ethics, Fairness, Safety & Governance</a></li>
<li><a href="ch47.html">47 &middot; 🌠 Frontier & Emerging Directions</a></li>
</ul>
</details>

------------------------------------------------------------------------

Anomaly detection is the discipline of finding the rare, the unexpected, and the suspicious — the fraudulent transaction hiding among millions of legitimate ones, the failing turbine bearing, the intruder on a network, the tumor on a scan. What sets it apart from ordinary classification is the scarcity of examples: you almost never have a clean, balanced library of "anomalies" to learn from, because anomalies are by definition rare, varied, and often never seen before. This chapter sits in the Applied ML Systems part of the field: it borrows tools from classical ML, deep learning, and statistics, but reframes them all around one stubborn problem — learning what "normal" looks like well enough to flag what isn't.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
🧭 **In context:** Applied ML Systems · used for fraud, intrusion, fault, and health monitoring · the one key idea: model *normal* densely, then flag whatever the model finds surprising.
:::

## 37.1 — What counts as an anomaly, and why labels are scarce

An **anomaly** (or outlier) is an observation that deviates so much from the others that it raises suspicion it was generated by a different mechanism. That definition, due to Hawkins, carries the whole field in one sentence: anomalies are not just "far away" points, they are points that look like they came from a *different process* — a fraudster instead of a shopper, a cracked bearing instead of a healthy one.

The defining difficulty is **class imbalance**. In a fraud dataset, genuine transactions might outnumber fraud by 1,000-to-1 or 100,000-to-1. Two consequences follow. First, a lazy classifier that predicts "normal" for everything scores 99.9% accuracy while catching zero fraud — accuracy is useless here (we return to this in 37.6). Second, you rarely have enough labelled anomalies to characterise the *anomaly* class at all; you may have many flavours of fraud and only a handful of each. So instead of learning "what fraud looks like," most methods learn "what normal looks like" and treat anything poorly explained by that model as suspect. This flips the usual supervised framing on its head and explains why so much of the field is unsupervised.

It also helps to name the *kinds* of anomaly, because the right method depends on the kind:

- **Point anomaly** — a single instance is odd on its own (a \$40,000 charge on a card that never exceeds \$200). The simplest and most common case.
- **Contextual anomaly** — a value is normal in general but odd *in its context*. 30°C is fine in July, alarming in January; the temperature is only anomalous given the season. Context is usually time, location, or a grouping key. The standard recipe: **subtract a seasonal or group baseline, then run an ordinary point detector on the residual** — the value minus what was expected for that context (more in 37.3 and 37.7, and Chapter 36 for the seasonal models).
- **Collective anomaly** — no single point is strange, but a *sequence or set* together is. A heartbeat pattern where each beat is plausible but the rhythm is wrong; a slow data exfiltration where each packet is ordinary but the aggregate flow is not. Here you cannot score points in isolation: the standard move is to **slide a window over the sequence and score the window** — by its reconstruction error under a model trained on normal sequences, or by the residual against a forecast (Chapter 36). The unit of detection becomes the window, not the point.

Notice that contextual and collective anomalies both reduce to a point detector once you do the right preprocessing: contextual by *subtracting* a baseline, collective by *grouping* into windows. The detectors in 37.3–37.5 are the engines; this framing is the plumbing that feeds them.

::: {.callout-warning}
"Anomaly" and "rare class" are not synonyms. A rare-but-known category (say, a specific rare disease you have many examples of) is an imbalanced *classification* problem. True anomaly detection applies when the anomalous class is ill-defined, open-ended, or unseen — you cannot enumerate it in advance.
:::

## 37.2 — Three framings: supervised, unsupervised, semi-supervised

How you attack the problem depends entirely on what labels you have, and it is worth being explicit because mixing these up is the most common modelling mistake.

**Supervised** detection assumes you have labelled examples of *both* normal and anomalous data. Then anomaly detection is just imbalanced binary classification, and you reach for the tools of Chapter 8 (ensembles), class weighting, resampling, and the threshold methods in 37.6. This is the rare luxury; use it when you genuinely have trustworthy anomaly labels.

**Semi-supervised** (often called *one-class* or *novelty* detection) assumes you have a clean sample of **normal** data only, and no anomalies at training time. You learn a boundary or density around normal and flag anything outside it. This matches reality surprisingly often: you can collect months of healthy-machine telemetry, but you cannot manufacture every possible failure. Most "train on normal, score everything" methods — one-class SVM, autoencoders — live here.

**Unsupervised** detection assumes *no labels at all* and that anomalies are rare and different. It scores every point by how much it stands out from the rest of the same dataset, with no separate training phase. Isolation Forest and LOF are the workhorses. The catch: it quietly assumes anomalies are a small minority, so if your "rare" event is actually 20% of the data, these methods break.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    A[What labels do you have?] -->|both classes labelled| B[Supervised:<br/>imbalanced classification]
    A -->|only normal labelled| C[Semi-supervised:<br/>one-class / novelty]
    A -->|no labels| D[Unsupervised:<br/>outlier scoring]
    B --> E[ensembles, class weights,<br/>PR-AUC threshold]
    C --> F[one-class SVM,<br/>autoencoder reconstruction]
    D --> G[Isolation Forest, LOF,<br/>DBSCAN]
```

A useful mental rule: the cleaner your notion of "normal," the more you should lean on semi-supervised methods; the murkier it is, the more you fall back to unsupervised scoring and accept more false alarms.

## 37.3 — Statistical methods: z-scores, Gaussians, and EWMA

The oldest and still most useful family asks a simple question: *under a model of normal, how improbable is this point?* If improbable enough, flag it.

Start with the **z-score**. Assume a feature is roughly bell-shaped with mean $\mu$ and standard deviation $\sigma$. The z-score measures how many standard deviations a point sits from the mean:

$$ z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma} $$

A common rule flags $|z| > 3$ (beyond three sigma, under a Gaussian, is about a 0.3% event). It is trivial to compute and easy to explain to a stakeholder, which matters more than it sounds.

Its weakness is subtle and worth dwelling on, because it is the central pitfall of the whole statistical family. Both $\mu$ and $\sigma$ are computed *from the same data that contains the outliers you are hunting*, and a single extreme value can wreck them. This has two named failure modes:

- **Masking** — one or more outliers inflate $\sigma$ (and drag $\mu$) so much that they pull the threshold out past themselves, so they no longer look anomalous and *hide each other*. A pair of frauds at \$50,000 each can make a \$50,000 charge look only two sigma out.
- **Swamping** — the same inflated spread makes *genuinely normal* points near the edge of the bulk look anomalous, drowning you in false positives.

The cure is **robust estimation**: statistics whose **breakdown point** — the fraction of the data you can corrupt before the estimate flies off to infinity — is high. The mean and standard deviation have a breakdown point of $0$ (one bad point in a million is enough to move them arbitrarily far). The **median** and the **MAD** (median absolute deviation, the median of $|x_i - \text{median}|$) have a breakdown point of $50\%$: you must corrupt half the data before they fail. The **modified z-score** swaps in exactly these robust pieces:

$$ z_{\text{mod}} = \frac{0.6745\,(x - \tilde{x})}{\text{MAD}}, \qquad \tilde{x} = \text{median}(x) $$

where the constant $0.6745$ rescales MAD so that, on clean Gaussian data, $z_{\text{mod}}$ matches the ordinary z-score (so the familiar "> 3" rule still applies). Because the median and MAD ignore the extreme tails, a handful of outliers can no longer mask themselves or swamp their neighbours. This is the single most important upgrade in practical statistical anomaly detection.

For several features at once, the **Mahalanobis distance** generalises the z-score to a multivariate Gaussian, measuring distance from the mean while accounting for how features co-vary:

$$ D_M(x) = \sqrt{(x-\mu)^\top \Sigma^{-1} (x-\mu)} $$

where $\Sigma$ is the covariance matrix. It correctly treats a point as anomalous if it violates the *correlation* structure even when each individual feature looks fine. (It inherits the same masking problem — $\mu$ and $\Sigma$ are non-robust — which is why robust covariance estimators like the Minimum Covariance Determinant exist.)

Time series need a *moving* baseline, because "normal" drifts. The **EWMA** (exponentially weighted moving average) keeps a running estimate that weights recent points more heavily:

$$ S_t = \alpha\, x_t + (1-\alpha)\, S_{t-1}, \qquad 0 < \alpha < 1 $$

You then flag points whose deviation from $S_t$ exceeds a band built from a similarly-smoothed variance. Small $\alpha$ means a slow, stable baseline; large $\alpha$ means it adapts fast but is jumpier. This is the backbone of classic control-chart monitoring and most lightweight production alerting, and it is also the natural engine for **contextual** anomalies: let $S_t$ be the expected value for the current context, and the residual $x_t - S_t$ is what you threshold.

::: {.callout-tip}
Reach for statistical methods first. They are fast, interpretable, need no training loop, and on low-dimensional or single-metric data they are often *all you need*. Add complexity only when they demonstrably miss things — and when you do reach for them, reach for the **robust** version (median/MAD) by default, not the textbook mean/$\sigma$ one.
:::

## 37.4 — Distance and density: kNN, LOF, and DBSCAN

When data has no clean parametric shape, let the geometry speak. The intuition: normal points sit in crowded neighbourhoods; anomalies sit alone in sparse regions.

The simplest version is **kNN distance**: score each point by its distance to its $k$-th nearest neighbour (or the average of its $k$ nearest). Big distance means isolated means anomalous. It is nonparametric and makes no distributional assumption — but it has one fatal blind spot. It uses a single global distance threshold, so it fails when normal density *varies across the space*: a point that is tight by the standards of a sparse cluster may look "far" globally, while a genuine outlier hugging a dense cluster slips through.

**LOF** (Local Outlier Factor) fixes exactly this by going *local*. Instead of raw distance, it compares each point's local density to the densities of its neighbours. Roughly:

$$ \text{LOF}(x) = \frac{\text{average local density of } x\text{'s neighbours}}{\text{local density of } x} $$

An LOF near 1 means the point is as dense as its surroundings (normal). Much greater than 1 means the point is in a relatively *sparser* pocket than its neighbours — a local outlier. By normalising against the neighbourhood, LOF catches outliers that sit on the fringe of one cluster even if another cluster is naturally sparse. The cost is sensitivity to $k$ and an $O(n^2)$ flavour on large data without indexing.

**DBSCAN** comes at it from clustering (see Chapter 9): it groups points that are *density-reachable* — within distance $\varepsilon$ of enough neighbours (`minPts`). Points that never join any dense group are labelled **noise**, and that noise label *is* your anomaly flag. It is a clean, threshold-free way to get outliers as a by-product of clustering, but you must choose $\varepsilon$ and `minPts`, and a single global $\varepsilon$ struggles with clusters of very different densities.

Here is the mechanism of the simplest member, made concrete:

```python
import numpy as np

def knn_anomaly_scores(X, k=5):
    # score = distance to the k-th nearest neighbour; bigger = more isolated
    n = len(X)
    scores = np.zeros(n)
    for i in range(n):
        d = np.linalg.norm(X - X[i], axis=1)  # distances to all points
        d.sort()                              # d[0] is self (0)
        scores[i] = d[k]                      # k-th neighbour distance
    return scores

# quick self-check: a planted far-away point must score highest
X = np.vstack([np.random.randn(50, 2), [[12, 12]]])
s = knn_anomaly_scores(X)
assert s.argmax() == len(X) - 1
```

::: {.callout-warning}
Distance and density methods live and die by **feature scaling**. A feature measured in dollars (range 0–50,000) will dwarf one measured 0–1, so the distance is effectively computed on the big feature alone. Standardise or normalise first (Chapter 4), or your "anomalies" are just an artefact of units.
:::

## 37.5 — Model-based detectors: Isolation Forest, one-class SVM, autoencoders

The previous methods *measure* normal. These three *build a model* of it, and they are the everyday tools of production anomaly detection.

**Isolation Forest** inverts the usual logic with a lovely trick: instead of profiling normal points, it tries to *isolate* each point by random splits, and notices that anomalies are easy to isolate. Build many random binary trees; at each node pick a random feature and a random split value. A point in a dense normal region needs many splits before it ends up alone in a leaf; an anomaly, sitting out in empty space, gets cut off after just a few. The **anomaly score** is built from the average path length to isolate the point — short path means anomalous. It is fast (roughly linear), handles high dimensions well, needs almost no tuning, and assumes nothing about distribution shape, which is why it is often the first model people try.

**One-class SVM** is the semi-supervised boundary method (kernels and SVMs are Chapter 7). Trained on *normal data only*, it learns a frontier that encloses the bulk of that data in feature space — using a kernel to wrap a tight, possibly non-convex boundary around the normal region. Anything falling outside the frontier at test time is an anomaly. It is powerful when you have a clean normal sample, but sensitive to its $\nu$ parameter (which sets the expected fraction of outliers, and also lower-bounds the fraction of points that become support vectors) and to scaling, and it slows down on very large datasets.

**Reconstruction-based** detection uses an **autoencoder** (Chapter 19): a neural network trained to compress input to a small bottleneck and reconstruct it. Train it only on normal data and it becomes an expert at rebuilding *normal* — and clumsy at anything else. So the **reconstruction error**, the gap between input and output, becomes the anomaly score:

$$ \text{score}(x) = \lVert x - \hat{x} \rVert^2, \qquad \hat{x} = \text{decode}(\text{encode}(x)) $$

A normal point reconstructs cleanly (low error); an anomaly the network never learned to represent reconstructs poorly (high error). You set a threshold on that error — commonly a high percentile of the errors seen on normal validation data. This approach shines on high-dimensional, structured inputs (images, multivariate sensor streams) where a hand-built density model is hopeless, and it is the natural engine for **collective** anomalies: feed it sliding windows of a sequence instead of single points, and a window with high reconstruction error is a rhythm or pattern gone wrong.

| Method | Framing | Assumes | Strengths | Watch out for |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Isolation Forest | unsup. | anomalies are few & separable | fast, scales, low tuning | weaker on local/contextual outliers |
| One-class SVM | semi-sup. | clean normal sample | flexible non-linear boundary | $\nu$ tuning, scaling, large-$n$ cost |
| Autoencoder | semi-sup. | normal is reconstructable | high-dim / structured / sequence data | needs data + training; can over-generalise |

::: {.callout-warning}
A model is only as honest as its training set. If anomalies leak into the "normal" training data, a one-class SVM or autoencoder will learn to treat them as normal and stay silent on exactly the cases you care about. Curate the normal set.
:::

## 37.6 — Evaluation under extreme imbalance, and setting the threshold

Most detectors output a continuous **anomaly score**, not a yes/no. Turning that score into a decision, and judging the result, is where naïve evaluation goes wrong.

The first rule: **ignore accuracy.** With 0.1% anomalies, "predict normal always" scores 99.9% and is worthless. The honest metrics are **precision** (of the points we flagged, how many were truly anomalies — i.e. how much alert-fatigue we cause) and **recall** (of the true anomalies, how many we caught — i.e. how many we missed):

$$ \text{Precision} = \frac{TP}{TP + FP}, \qquad \text{Recall} = \frac{TP}{TP + FN} $$

These trade off against each other as you move the threshold, and a single operating point hides that trade-off. The **PR-AUC** (area under the precision–recall curve) summarises performance across *all* thresholds and is the right headline metric under heavy imbalance. Prefer it to **ROC-AUC** here: ROC's false-positive rate has a giant negative class in its denominator, so it stays flatteringly high even when precision is dismal. The precision–recall curve, by contrast, exposes the pain directly.

<svg viewBox="0 0 260 180" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" font-family="sans-serif" font-size="11">
  <line x1="40" y1="150" x2="240" y2="150" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1"/>
  <line x1="40" y1="20" x2="40" y2="150" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="1"/>
  <text x="140" y="172" text-anchor="middle">Recall</text>
  <text x="14" y="90" text-anchor="middle" transform="rotate(-90 14 90)">Precision</text>
  <path d="M40,30 C110,34 150,70 175,105 S220,148 240,150" fill="none" stroke="currentColor" stroke-width="2"/>
  <circle cx="175" cy="105" r="3" fill="currentColor"/>
  <text x="180" y="100">chosen threshold</text>
</svg>

Choosing the operating threshold is a *business* decision, not a statistical one, because the costs are lopsided. A missed fraud (false negative) might cost thousands; a false alarm (false positive) costs an analyst five minutes. Translate that into the metric you optimise:

- If missing anomalies is catastrophic (intrusion, medical), favour **recall** — lower the threshold, accept more false alarms.
- If false alarms are expensive and self-defeating (alert fatigue silences real alerts), favour **precision** — raise the threshold.
- The **$F_\beta$ score** lets you dial the balance: $\beta > 1$ weights recall, $\beta < 1$ weights precision.

A practical default when you lack labels: set the threshold at a percentile of the score distribution that matches your alert *budget*. If analysts can review 50 cases a day, threshold so that roughly 50 fire. It is crude, but it aligns the model with the human capacity that actually consumes its output.

::: {.callout-tip}
Always evaluate on a **time-split** for temporal data: train on the past, test on the future. Randomly shuffling rows lets information leak from future to past and produces gorgeous offline numbers that collapse in production.
:::

## 37.7 — Streaming and online detection

Much of anomaly detection runs not on a static table but on a *live feed* — card swipes, log lines, sensor ticks — where you must decide *now*, before the next point arrives, and you cannot store everything. This changes the constraints more than the ideas.

Two demands dominate. First, **bounded memory and compute**: you process each point once, in roughly constant time and space, so re-fitting a model over the whole history every tick is out. Second, **concept drift**: "normal" itself moves — traffic has daily and weekly cycles, user behaviour shifts, sensors age. A detector frozen on last month's normal will either drown you in false alarms or go blind. The EWMA of 37.3 is the canonical streaming primitive precisely because it updates in one line and forgets the distant past automatically. Streaming variants of Isolation Forest (e.g. windowed or half-space trees) and online clustering apply the same adapt-as-you-go principle.

Drift forces a genuine tension. Adapt too slowly and you cry wolf after every legitimate shift; adapt too fast and a slow attack becomes the new "normal" and is absorbed silently. The usual compromise is a **sliding window** or exponential forgetting tuned so the baseline tracks benign drift (a holiday traffic ramp) but not events on the timescale of the anomalies you care about.

```python
def ewma_stream(stream, alpha=0.3, k=3.0, warmup=10):
    # one-pass detector: flag points far from a moving mean,
    # measured in units of a moving standard deviation.
    # ponytail: cold-start is real — z-scores before `warmup` points
    # are unreliable because mean/var haven't stabilised. We seed
    # mean/var from the first `warmup` points and emit no flags there.
    buf, mean, var = [], None, None
    for x in stream:
        if mean is None:
            buf.append(x)
            if len(buf) < warmup:          # still warming up: never flag
                yield x, False
                continue
            mean = sum(buf) / len(buf)     # seed from warm-up window
            var = sum((b - mean) ** 2 for b in buf) / len(buf)
            yield x, False
            continue
        z = abs(x - mean) / (var ** 0.5 + 1e-9)
        flag = z > k                       # decide BEFORE updating
        if not flag:                       # update only on clean points
            diff = x - mean
            mean += alpha * diff
            # EWMA variance recursion: sigma^2_t = (1-a)(sigma^2_{t-1} + a*diff^2)
            var = (1 - alpha) * (var + alpha * diff * diff)
        yield x, flag

data = [10, 10, 10, 10, 10, 10, 10, 10, 10, 10, 99, 10, 10]  # spike after warm-up
flags = [f for _, f in ewma_stream(data)]
assert flags[10] and sum(flags) == 1       # exactly the spike fires
```

Two deliberate choices in that code carry the streaming lesson. First, the **warm-up**: the first several z-scores are computed against a mean and variance that have not yet stabilised, so seeding them from a small window and suppressing flags until then avoids arbitrary alarms on the opening points — a cold-start that naive implementations get wrong. Second, the baseline updates **only on points judged normal**, so a spike does not contaminate the very mean used to detect the next one. That guard — score first, then update only if clean — is the streaming-specific pitfall most implementations miss.

## 37.x — Key takeaways

- Anomalies are points generated by a *different process*; the central obstacle is extreme imbalance and the scarcity (or absence) of anomaly labels, so most methods model **normal** and flag the surprising.
- Match the framing to your labels: **supervised** (both classes), **semi-supervised / one-class** (normal only), **unsupervised** (no labels). Mislabelling this is the most common error.
- Distinguish **point**, **contextual**, and **collective** anomalies — the kind dictates the plumbing: contextual reduces to *baseline subtraction then a point detector*, collective to *windowing then reconstruction or forecast residual*.
- Climb the ladder of methods: **statistical** (z-score, modified z-score, Mahalanobis, EWMA) first; then **distance/density** (kNN, LOF, DBSCAN); then **model-based** (Isolation Forest, one-class SVM, autoencoder reconstruction error).
- Prefer **robust** statistics (median, MAD) over mean/$\sigma$: their high **breakdown point** defeats **masking** (outliers hiding each other) and **swamping** (normal points flagged), the two failure modes of naive z-scores.
- Scale your features — distance and density methods are meaningless on unscaled data.
- **Never use accuracy.** Report **precision**, **recall**, and **PR-AUC**; choose the threshold from asymmetric business costs (or your alert budget), and evaluate on a time-split.
- Streaming detection adds bounded compute and **concept drift**: adapt the baseline fast enough to track benign change but slow enough not to absorb the attack — and watch the cold-start, scoring before updating only on clean points.

## 37.y — See also

- **Chapter 04 — Data Preprocessing & Feature Engineering** — scaling and feature construction that make distance/density methods work.
- **Chapter 09 — Clustering & Unsupervised Learning** — DBSCAN and the density ideas underpinning unsupervised detection.
- **Chapter 11 — Model Evaluation, Validation & Selection** — precision/recall, PR vs ROC curves, and threshold selection in depth.
- **Chapter 07 — Support Vector Machines & Kernels** — the machinery behind one-class SVM.
- **Chapter 19 — Autoencoders & Self-Supervised Learning** — reconstruction-based detection, the engine for collective/windowed anomalies.
- **Chapter 36 — Time Series Analysis & Forecasting** — contextual and collective anomalies via seasonality and forecast-residual detection.
- **Chapter 12 — Bayesian Machine Learning** — probabilistic density models for principled "how surprising is this?" scoring.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

[📖 All chapters](../ai-ml-encyclopedia.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [← 36 · ⏳ Time Series Analysis & Forecasting](ch36.html)  &nbsp;|&nbsp;  [38 · 🧭 Search & Problem Solving →](ch38.html)
